raw_response: {"simulated_user_message":"I think playing the piano helped because it gave me a structured outlet where I could focus on rhythm instead of my own self‑criticism; the act of hitting keys felt like a way to prove my competence internally, which eased some of my anxiety about the presentation. But right now, even that thought feels like a reminder that I’m not good enough, so I’m not sure if I can bring it back. Also, when I play I notice how my body relaxes, so it was a small way to counter my body‑image worries.","problems_referred":["general_anxiety","perfectionism","body_image_concerns"]}

parsed.simulated_user_message: I think playing the piano helped because it gave me a structured outlet where I could focus on rhythm instead of my own self‑criticism; the act of hitting keys felt like a way to prove my competence internally, which eased some of my anxiety about the presentation. But right now, even that thought feels like a reminder that I’m not good enough, so I’m not sure if I can bring it back. Also, when I play I notice how my body relaxes, so it was a small way to counter my body‑image worries.

parsed.final_response: It sounds like playing the piano was a really powerful way for you to calm your nerves and feel more confident before presentations, and it's interesting that it wasn't just about the music itself, but about the sense of control and competence it gave you. Yeah, it can be really tough to feel like you're losing the things that normally help you cope, especially when you're already feeling exhausted and uncertain. I'm wondering, what do you think it would take for you to feel like you could bring back some of that sense of calm and confidence, even if it's just for a few moments?
